content.text: The Gap Between Discovery and Delivery

Discovery workshops generate energy and shared understanding. Without a structured hand-off, that energy dissipates between the session and the first sprint. The team arrives at sprint planning with a raw board export, conflicting memories of decisions made verbally, and no agreement on what is safe to build. The discovery-to-backlog handoff criteria exist to close this gap.

: objective tests that must pass before a discovery output is considered ready for sprint planning. They are not bureaucracy — they are the minimum set of questions that, if left unanswered, will generate follow-up during the sprint when the cost of clarification is much higher.
A backlog item that cannot be estimated is a risk disguised as a story. The hand-off gate forces that risk into the open before the sprint clock starts.

attrs.alt: Discovery-to-backlog gate model: four sequential decision gates (Completeness, Hot Spots Actioned, Stories Estimable, Legal/Security Sign-Off) that must all pass before a discovery output enters sprint planning

content.text: The diagram above shows the four gates a discovery output package must clear before it enters sprint planning. Each gate has a clearly defined pass condition and a prescribed remediation path on failure. Gates are checked in order — failing Gate 1 means the later gates are not evaluated until the missing artefacts are delivered.

Gate 1 verifies that all required artefacts from the discovery workshop exist and are committed to the repository. A missing artefact is returned to the facilitator; sprint planning is not scheduled until the package is complete.
Markdown table with Event / Trigger / Payload / Notes columns
At least one event per bounded context identified in the session
Each bounded context has a name, responsibility statement, and at least one known API boundary
Markdown table of hot spots → risk entries
Every red hot-spot note from the session appears as a row
GitHub Issues or Jira epics with user stories
One epic per bounded context minimum; each has at least one acceptance criterion
One DR per decision made during the narrative walk
At least 3 cases per bounded context
PNG photos or JSON export in docs/discovery/archive/
Uploaded within 2 hours of session close

import os
from pathlib import Path

def check_discovery_completeness(
    feature_slug: str,
    docs_root: str = "docs"
) -> dict[str, bool]:
    """
    Check that all required discovery artefacts exist for a feature.
    Returns a dict of {artefact_name: exists}.
    """
    base = Path(docs_root)
    checks = {
        "domain_event_glossary": (base / "discovery" / feature_slug / "domain-event-glossary.md").exists(),
        "bounded_context_map":   (base / "discovery" / feature_slug / "context-map.png").exists()
                               or (base / "discovery" / feature_slug / "context-map.svg").exists(),
        "risk_log":              (base / "risks" / f"{feature_slug}-risk-log.md").exists(),
        "board_archive":         any((base / "discovery" / feature_slug / "archive").glob("*.png"))
                               or any((base / "discovery" / feature_slug / "archive").glob("*.json")),
        "decision_records":      any((base / "decisions").glob(f"*{feature_slug}*.md")),
        "eval_seeds":            any(Path("evals/seeds").glob(f"{feature_slug}*.json")),
        "backlog_epics":         _github_epics_exist(feature_slug),  # see implementation below
    }
    return checks

def _github_epics_exist(feature_slug: str) -> bool:
    """Check GitHub Issues API for epics tagged with the feature slug."""
    import urllib.request, json
    repo = os.environ.get("GITHUB_REPOSITORY", "")
    token = os.environ.get("GITHUB_TOKEN", "")
    if not repo or not token:
        return False  # cannot check without credentials
    url = f"https://api.github.com/repos/{repo}/issues?labels=event-storming,{feature_slug}&state=open"
    req = urllib.request.Request(url, headers={"Authorization": f"token {token}"})
    with urllib.request.urlopen(req) as r:
        issues = json.loads(r.read())
    return len(issues) >= 1

def gate_1_report(feature_slug: str) -> str:
    results = check_discovery_completeness(feature_slug)
    passed = all(results.values())
    lines = [f"Gate 1 — Completeness {'PASS' if passed else 'FAIL'}", ""]
    for artefact, exists in results.items():
        status = "✓" if exists else "✗ MISSING"
        lines.append(f"  {status}  {artefact.replace('_', ' ').title()}")
    if not passed:
        missing = [k for k, v in results.items() if not v]
        lines.append(f"\nReturn to facilitator: {', '.join(missing)}")
    return "\n".join(lines)
Gate 2 verifies that every red hot-spot note from the discovery session has been actioned. 'Actioned' does not mean 'resolved' — it means a named person has committed to a next step with a deadline.
Valid Actions for a Hot Spot
Title, acceptance criterion ('spike produces a decision'), owner, sprint target, max story points (≤ 5)
The team has enough information to decide now
DR-NNN.md committed to docs/decisions/ with rationale and consequences
The hot spot is real but out of scope for this release
Written rationale in risk log; named owner; review trigger date
Hot spot can be resolved by adding detail to the bounded context map
Updated diagram committed; no separate story needed

## Hot Spot Triage — [Feature Name] — [Date]

For each red note from the session, complete this checklist:

| # | Hot Spot Text | Action Type | Owner | Due Date | Done? |
|---|--------------|-------------|-------|----------|-------|
| 1 | | | | | [ ] |
| 2 | | | | | [ ] |
| ...| | | | | |

Gate 2 PASSES when every row in the Done? column is checked.
Gate 2 FAILS if any row has no Action Type or no Owner.

Gate 3 applies the '13-point test': any backlog item that the team cannot estimate at ≤ 13 story points in a planning poker round is either split or replaced by a spike story. Items that cannot be sized signal unresolved design questions — the same class of problem as an unactioned hot spot.
When an epic cannot be sized, apply one of the three standard split patterns for Claude features:
: if the epic spans multiple bounded contexts, split it one story per context. Each story now lives within a single consistency boundary and can be sized independently.

: if the epic involves both a fast classification step and a slower generation step, split into 'Intent Classification' and 'Response Generation' stories. Different model tiers often have different latency and failure profiles, making separate stories easier to size.

: the happy path story covers normal inputs. A second story handles the identified failure modes (guardrail trips, empty context, timeout recovery). Edge case stories are almost always smaller and can be deferred to a later sprint.
Using Claude to Surface Sizing Blockers

import anthropic, json

def identify_sizing_blockers(
    epic_title: str,
    epic_description: str,
    acceptance_criteria: list[str],
    team_estimate: int  # story points from planning poker
) -> str:
    """
    If a team cannot estimate an epic (or estimates it at > 13 points),
    use Claude to identify what information is missing.
    """
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=800,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"""
Epic title: {epic_title}
Description: {epic_description}
Acceptance criteria:
{json.dumps(acceptance_criteria, indent=2)}
Team estimate: {team_estimate} story points

This epic {'cannot be estimated' if team_estimate == 0 else f'was estimated at {team_estimate} points (above the 13-point gate)'}.

Identify:
1. The 2-3 most likely reasons it cannot be sized confidently.
2. The specific questions that, if answered, would allow the team to split it into stories of ≤ 8 points.
3. Whether a spike story would resolve the uncertainty (and if so, what the spike should investigate).

Be concise and actionable."""}]
    )
    return response.content[0].text

# Example
blockers = identify_sizing_blockers(
    epic_title="Streaming response with tool calls",
    epic_description="The assistant should stream its response token-by-token while concurrently executing MCP tool calls.",
    acceptance_criteria=[
        "Given a query requiring a tool call, When the user submits it, Then tokens stream within 500 ms of the tool result being available",
        "Given a tool call failure, When the tool errors, Then the assistant gracefully degrades to a no-tool response",
    ],
    team_estimate=0  # team could not estimate
)
print(blockers)

Gate 4 verifies that the risk log has been reviewed by Legal and Security and that no HIGH-probability / HIGH-impact risk remains open without a mitigation plan. This gate exists because sprint planning is not the right forum for discovering that a feature requires a DPIA or that a planned data flow violates a data-residency requirement.
Risk Classification for the Sign-Off Review
Conversation history retention, user ID in prompts, PII in tool call responses
Hallucination in high-stakes domain (medical, legal, financial)
RAG over documents with unclear copyright status, OSS licence of embedded tools
Cryptographic algorithms in MCP tools, dual-use capabilities
Anthropic API rate limits, availability SLAs against contractual commitments
Prompt injection via tool results, secrets in system prompt, SSRF via MCP calls

# Discovery Hand-off Sign-Off — [Feature Name]
**Discovery session date:** [date]
**Sprint planning target:** [sprint date]

## Gate Checklist

| Gate | Condition | Status | Signed Off By | Date |
|------|-----------|--------|---------------|------|
| 1 — Completeness | All 7 artefacts committed to repo | | | |
| 2 — Hot Spots | All red notes actioned (spike / DR / deferral) | | | |
| 3 — Estimable | All epics ≤ 13 points or split | | | |
| 4 — Legal / Sec | No open HIGH/HIGH risks | | | |

## Conditional Approvals
List any features or stories that are approved conditional on a spike result:
- Story [ID]: approved if spike [ID] concludes [condition].

## Open Items (Explicitly Deferred)
List hot spots deferred to a future release, with rationale:
- [Hot spot text] — deferred because [rationale] — review by [date].

**Overall status:** [ ] READY FOR SPRINT PLANNING  [ ] ON HOLD (see open items above)
The hand-off process has strict timing requirements. Slack in the timeline allows problems discovered at Gate 3 or 4 to be resolved without delaying the sprint.
Synthesis (glossary, risk log, DR, epics, eval seeds)
What Happens When a Gate Fails

A gate failure is not a blocker — it is a signal to do specific work before planning. The prescribed remediation paths in the gate diagram above specify exactly what work is required. The product manager owns gate status and communicates it to the scrum master; if Gate 4 is still pending 24 hours before sprint planning, the sprint date is pushed by one week. This rule should be explicit in the team's working agreement so it is not a surprise when it fires.
Do not override the gates under delivery pressure
. A feature that enters a sprint before Gate 4 is cleared will generate an ad-hoc legal review during the sprint, which interrupts velocity and often results in a partial revert anyway.
. A recurring Gate 2 failure (hot spots consistently un-actioned by T+30 hours) is a process problem, not a content problem. Retrospect on it.

. Gates 3 and 4 can often run in parallel — the legal reviewer does not need the estimation session to conclude, and the engineering team does not need legal sign-off to estimate stories. The product manager coordinates.
Connecting Hand-Off Criteria to Definition of Ready

Many agile teams define a 'Definition of Ready' (DoR) — the conditions a story must meet before it can enter a sprint. The four gate criteria above translate directly into a DoR tailored for Claude features. Maintaining a feature-specific DoR alongside the generic team DoR prevents the gates from being re-negotiated each sprint.

## Definition of Ready — Claude Features

A backlog item is ready for sprint planning when ALL of the following are true:

### Discovery Origin
- [ ] Item originates from a discovery session (session date and board URL recorded in issue description)
- [ ] The bounded context this item belongs to is named in the issue

### Clarity
- [ ] User story follows the "As a [actor] I want [action] so that [outcome]" format
- [ ] At least 3 Gherkin acceptance criteria (Given/When/Then) are written
- [ ] The specific Claude model tier (Haiku / Sonnet / Opus) targeted is named, or a spike exists to determine it

### Sizing
- [ ] Team has completed planning poker; consensus estimate is ≤ 13 story points
- [ ] If estimate was > 13, the item has been split and this issue represents one slice ≤ 13 points

### Risk & Compliance
- [ ] All risk log entries linked to this item have: owner + mitigation + status
- [ ] No open HIGH/HIGH risk is linked to this item without an approved mitigation plan
- [ ] If item processes personal data: DPIA trigger assessment completed (linked)

### Test Coverage
- [ ] At least 1 eval seed case exists in evals/seeds/ for each acceptance criterion
- [ ] Eval type (exact_match / llm_judge / human_review) specified for each case

### Dependencies
- [ ] All upstream dependencies are named and their completion is not blocking this sprint
- [ ] Any MCP tool servers required are available in the target environment
The hand-off criteria are only valuable if the team measures whether they are working. Two metrics are most informative: gate pass rates and sprint carry-over rates.

Track what percentage of discovery outputs pass each gate on first submission (without requiring remediation). A healthy programme targets ≥ 85% first-pass for Gates 1–3 and ≥ 70% for Gate 4 (legal reviews often surface new information even on well-prepared packages).

import anthropic, json
from datetime import datetime

def generate_handoff_quality_report(
    gate_results: list[dict],  # list of {feature, gate_1, gate_2, gate_3, gate_4, sprint_carryover_pct}
    period: str
) -> str:
    """Generate a quality trend report for discovery hand-offs using Claude."""
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()

    # Compute pass rates
    total = len(gate_results)
    pass_rates = {}
    for gate in ["gate_1", "gate_2", "gate_3", "gate_4"]:
        passed = sum(1 for r in gate_results if r.get(gate) == "pass")
        pass_rates[gate] = round(passed / max(total, 1) * 100, 1)

    avg_carryover = sum(r.get("sprint_carryover_pct", 0) for r in gate_results) / max(total, 1)

    prompt = f"""You are an Engineering Process Manager reviewing discovery hand-off quality for {period}.

Data:
- Total features reviewed: {total}
- Gate 1 (Completeness) first-pass rate: {pass_rates['gate_1']}% (target: 85%)
- Gate 2 (Hot Spots) first-pass rate: {pass_rates['gate_2']}% (target: 85%)
- Gate 3 (Estimable) first-pass rate: {pass_rates['gate_3']}% (target: 85%)
- Gate 4 (Legal/Sec) first-pass rate: {pass_rates['gate_4']}% (target: 70%)
- Average sprint carry-over: {avg_carryover:.1f}% (target: <10%)

Write a 4-bullet executive summary highlighting:
1. Which gate is the biggest bottleneck
2. Whether carry-over is correlated with gate failures
3. One concrete process improvement recommendation
4. Whether the programme is trending better or worse vs targets

Be direct and data-driven.""".strip()

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=600,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return response.content[0].text

# Example
report = generate_handoff_quality_report(
    gate_results=[
        {"feature": "query-assistant", "gate_1": "pass", "gate_2": "fail", "gate_3": "pass", "gate_4": "pass", "sprint_carryover_pct": 15},
        {"feature": "doc-summariser", "gate_1": "pass", "gate_2": "pass", "gate_3": "pass", "gate_4": "pass", "sprint_carryover_pct": 5},
        {"feature": "hr-chatbot", "gate_1": "fail", "gate_2": "pass", "gate_3": "fail", "gate_4": "fail", "sprint_carryover_pct": 30},
    ],
    period="Q2 2025"
)
print(report)

Sprint carry-over (stories not completed within the committed sprint) is the downstream consequence of a failed hand-off. Tracking carry-over by discovery origin reveals whether the gate process is improving delivery predictability. A story that entered the sprint having failed Gate 3 is three times more likely to carry over than one that cleared all four gates — this signal justifies the process overhead to sceptical stakeholders.
Scope grows during sprint as hidden complexity emerges
Design questions reopen during sprint, triggering unplanned design sessions
Adapting the Gates for Fast-Moving Teams
Teams running two-week sprints with weekly discovery sessions may find the 48-hour gate timeline tight. Two adaptations preserve the intent without sacrificing velocity.

Rather than batching all gates at T+48 hours, teams can check gates as artefacts arrive. Gate 1 is checked when synthesis is submitted (T+24h); Gate 2 is checked in a 30-minute async triage (T+30h); Gate 3 is checked during a standing refinement session (T+36h, already in the sprint cadence); Gate 4 has a standing Legal/Security 'rapid review' slot every Thursday morning for features targeting the following sprint.

Not all Claude features carry equal legal and security risk. Teams can define a 'lightweight Gate 4' for features with no new data flows, no PII, no new external integrations, and no model-tier changes. For these features, Gate 4 is a self-certification by the product manager against a checklist, without requiring a synchronous Legal/Security review. The full Gate 4 review is triggered automatically if any of the four conditions is not met.

## Lightweight Gate 4 Self-Certification

Feature: ____________________  Date: __________  PM: ____________________

Certify ALL of the following for lightweight Gate 4 to apply:

[ ] No new personal data flows introduced or modified
[ ] No PII processed in prompts, tool calls, or stored outputs
[ ] No new third-party integrations or external API calls
[ ] Same Claude model tier as existing production features (no tier upgrade)

If any box is unchecked → full Gate 4 required. Escalate to Legal and Security.

Signature: ____________________  Date: __________
Treating the gate as a checkbox exercise

: the purpose of the gate is to surface blockers before planning, not to generate paperwork. If a gate is passing consistently but sprint carry-over remains high, the team is checking boxes without understanding the intent.
Legal review as a blocker rather than a collaborator

: framing Gate 4 as 'waiting for Legal approval' creates an adversarial dynamic. Frame it as 'Legal joins the hand-off review to advise on mitigation options' — the outcome is the same but the relationship is constructive.
Not updating the risk log during the sprint
: risks change as implementation reveals new information. The risk log should be a living document updated at each sprint review, not a snapshot frozen at planning time.

: without tracking pass rates and carry-over correlation, the team has no evidence to refine the process. Five minutes of data entry per feature produces the metrics that justify the process to leadership.

The discovery-to-backlog hand-off criteria define four sequential gates that a discovery output package must clear before sprint planning: completeness (all seven artefacts committed), hot spots actioned (every red note has an owner and next step), stories estimable (no epic above 13 story points without a spike), and legal/security sign-off (no unmitigated high-risk items). Each gate has a prescribed remediation path on failure and a defined owner and timing. The process completes within 48 hours of the discovery session, ensuring that sprint planning is grounded in a fully reviewed, legally cleared, and size-verified backlog — not in the raw output of an energetic workshop.
